coredata.dc:title: Photodynamic therapy using graphene quantum dot derivatives
coredata.dc:creator: Ahirwar, Satyaprakash
Mallick, Sudhanshu
Bahadur, Dhirendra
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we show photodynamic therapy (PDT) by graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) to kill the cancer cells. For PDT, we used GQDs and GOQDs as photosensitizer and 365 ​nm UV tubelight as irradiation source. GQDs/GOQDs are synthesized by electrochemical exfoliation of graphite rod. The electrolyte used was a combination of citric acid, NaOH and H2O2 in different proportion. The GQDs and GOQDs with the size range of 1.5–5.5 ​nm, show excitation dependent PL behavior. GQDs/GOQDs show strong absorbance in UV region, exhibit excitation dependent photoluminescence behavior ranging from UV to visible region and show singlet oxygen generation. GQDs/GOQDs show very low cytotoxicity towards skin cancer (B16F10) cells and breast cancer (MCF-7) cells. GQDs/GOQDs show easy cellular uptake and are well distributed inside the cells. Singlet oxygen generation, low cytotoxicity and easy cellular uptake make them a promising candidate for PDT application. PDT is performed on B16F10 ​cells and MCF-7 ​cells using GQDs/GOQDs. More than 90% cells are killed during a short exposure of 5 ​min. However, the cells not treated with GQDs/GOQDs and exposed to irradiation for 5 ​min show that more than 80% cells are alive. The GQD/GOQD PDT has several advantages such as high cell killing efficacy with micro-watt power UV irradiation, less irradiation time and uniform large area exposure. However, the therapy will be limited to skin related cancers as the depth of penetration of UV irradiation is very less.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photodynamic therapy
Graphene quantum dots
Graphene oxide quantum dots
Electrochemical exfoliation
Singlet oxygen

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In-vitro cytotoxicity measurement by SRB assay
Cytotoxicity of GQDs/GOQDs on cancer cells
Cellular uptake of GQDs/GOQDs on cancer cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Government of Kerala

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We would like to express acknowledgment for the financial support by the Wadhwani Research Center for Bioengineering (WRCB) IIT Bombay and the Nanomission, Department of Science and Technology (DST), Government of India. We would like to acknowledge Industrial Research and Consultancy Center (IRCC) Central Facility, IIT Bombay; Sophisticated Analytical Instrument Facility (SAIF), IIT Bombay for TEM, Raman, ESR and Confocal spectroscopy facility; Center of Excellence in Nanoelectronics (CEN), IIT Bombay for XPS facility. We thank Prof. A. Datta, Department of Chemistry, IIT Bombay, for TCSPC facility. We acknowledge Fiji software for confocal image analysis. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XPS, XRD and Raman spectra of GQD1. (a) XPS survey spectrum. Binding energy for C1s and O1s is observed at 284 ​eV and 531.2 ​eV respectively. (b) Magnified C1s XPS spectrum shows deconvoluted peaks for the binding energies of C–C, CC, C–O and CO. (c) XRD spectrum of GQD1 show peak at ca. 9⁰ and 24⁰. (d) Raman spectrum of GQD1 show D peak at 1351 ​cm−1 and G peak at 1585 ​cm−1 with ID/IG of 1.02.

TEM, UV-VIS, PLE, PL and TCSPC of GQD1. (a) TEM image shows GQD1 of different size. The size distribution curve shows GQD1 are in the range of 1.5–5.5 ​nm. The inset image is a high resolution image, in which separation between two layers is 0.22 ​nm. (b) UV-VIS spectra shows a peak at 253 ​nm and a shoulder peak at 365 ​nm. PL excitation spectra shows two peaks at 358 and 374 ​nm. Inset images show daylight image and the image taken with 365 ​nm UV lamp. (c) Excitation dependent PL. Inset shows excitation wavelength and corresponding emission wavelength. (d) Fluorescence decay profile and corresponding fitted profile. GQD1 was excited with 375 ​nm and emission was recorded at 450 ​nm.

Detection of singlet oxygen generation by ESR spectroscopy and detection of other reactive oxygen species by DCFDA dye. ESR spectra of (a) GQD1, (b) GQD2 and (c) GQD3 for different time intervals. Addition of NaN3 (singlet oxygen quenching agent) shows a significant reduction in ESR signal. DCFDA dye assay for (d) GQD1, (e) GQD2 and (f) GQD3.
Cytotoxicity of (a) GQD1, (b) GQD2 and (c) GQD3 on B16F10 cells. Cytotoxicity of (d) GQD1, (e) GQD2 and (f) GQD3 on MCF-7 cells.

In vitro time dependent cellular uptake of B16F10 ​cells incubated with (a) GQD1 (0.23 ​μM) and (b) GQD2 (1.07 ​μM) and (b) GQD3 (0.545 ​μM) for 2 ​h, (scale bar: 50 ​μm). All three images show bright field images, fluorescence images and merged images for different time intervals.
PDT for different time intervals on B16F10 ​cells using (a) GQD1, (b) GQD2 and (c) GQD3. PDT for different time intervals on MCF-7 ​cells using (d) GQD1, (e) GQD2 and (f) GQD3.
PDT results of GQD1, GQD2 and GQD3 on B16F10 and MCF-7 cancer cells.

head.ce:title: Photodynamic therapy using graphene quantum dot derivatives

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Metallurgical Engineering and Materials Science, Indian Institute of Technology Bombay, Mumbai, 400076, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Metallurgical Engineering and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Metallurgical Engineering and Materials Science, Indian Institute of Technology Bombay, Mumbai, 400076, India

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Present Address: Department of Chemical and Materials Engineering, IIT Goa, Ponda, Goa, 403401.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we show photodynamic therapy (PDT) by graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) to kill the cancer cells. For PDT, we used GQDs and GOQDs as photosensitizer and 365 ​nm UV tubelight as irradiation source. GQDs/GOQDs are synthesized by electrochemical exfoliation of graphite rod. The electrolyte used was a combination of citric acid, NaOH and H2O2 in different proportion. The GQDs and GOQDs with the size range of 1.5–5.5 ​nm, show excitation dependent PL behavior. GQDs/GOQDs show strong absorbance in UV region, exhibit excitation dependent photoluminescence behavior ranging from UV to visible region and show singlet oxygen generation. GQDs/GOQDs show very low cytotoxicity towards skin cancer (B16F10) cells and breast cancer (MCF-7) cells. GQDs/GOQDs show easy cellular uptake and are well distributed inside the cells. Singlet oxygen generation, low cytotoxicity and easy cellular uptake make them a promising candidate for PDT application. PDT is performed on B16F10 ​cells and MCF-7 ​cells using GQDs/GOQDs. More than 90% cells are killed during a short exposure of 5 ​min. However, the cells not treated with GQDs/GOQDs and exposed to irradiation for 5 ​min show that more than 80% cells are alive. The GQD/GOQD PDT has several advantages such as high cell killing efficacy with micro-watt power UV irradiation, less irradiation time and uniform large area exposure. However, the therapy will be limited to skin related cancers as the depth of penetration of UV irradiation is very less.

Figure caption: Cells before and after photodynamic therapy, bar chart for cell viability for different time intervals.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is one of the most dreaded diseases. It has been noticed that worldwide 8.8 million deaths are by cancer every year [1]. There are several possible methods for treating cancer such as surgery, chemotherapy, radiation therapy, hyperthermia, chemo-photothermal therapy, photodynamic therapy (PDT), etc. [2–5] PDT got attention because it is non-invasive and site specific photoactivation of photosensitizer drug and has very less side effects in comparison to the other therapies [6]. In PDT, the photosensitizer is activated by absorption of light of a specific wavelength. Then, the photosensitizer is transformed from ground state to excited state. The photosensitizer in the excited state reacts with molecular oxygen and converts the molecular oxygen into reactive oxygen species. These reactive oxygen species kill the cancer cells [7]. The photosensitizer in the excited state follows two types of mechanisms known as Type-I and Type-II mechanism. The Type-I mechanism involves transfer of hydrogen atom to form free radicals. The free radicals interact with molecular oxygen and convert it into oxygen radicals. In the Type-II mechanism, transfer of energy takes place between the photosensitizer in the excited state and ground state molecular oxygen known as triplet oxygen (3O2). The transfer of energy results in the formation of singlet oxygen (1O2). The singlet oxygen has a very short life time and reacts quickly with biomolecules, thereby causing damage to cancer cells [8].

Common photosensitizers used in PDT are porphyrins, chlorins, dyes, etc. [9,10] These photosensitizers have several drawbacks such as, they are generally hydrophobic in nature, requires high dose and exhibits low selectivity, high cellular uptake time, long duration required for removal from the body, prolonged skin photosensitivity, strong tendency for aggregation in solution, loss of photo-chemical activity, etc. [5] To reduce these effects, conjugates of photosensitizers with graphene oxide (GO) are used for PDT such as GO–PEG–Ce6 and GO–Pluronic–MB, etc. [11,12] Advantages of GO include its possible hydrophilic nature and a lot of functional groups on the surface where photosensitizer drug can be loaded. However, the GO conjugates are less efficient because GO sheet size is large in size, and photosensitizer drug loading and release efficiency is therefore very less [11,12]. With the current challenges and limitations, there is a need to develop more efficient and feasible alternatives that are able to produce high singlet oxygen generation yield, good solubility in physiological conditions, photostability, high tumor selectivity and biocompatibility for efficient PDT [13].

Excellent photoluminescence (PL) properties make GQDs a better candidate for PDT. The GQDs show high dispersibility in water, excellent biocompatibility, easy internalization into the cells and high singlet oxygen generation yield. The GQDs are used as autophagy, in which reactive oxygen species are generated when irradiated with 470 ​nm blue light and killing the U251 ​cells by causing oxidative stress [14]. A GQD PDT agent with high singlet oxygen generation yield, the Multi-state sensitization mechanism (MSS: S1 → T then T → S0) is shown to have two times singlet oxygen generation yield. This is observed in case of GQDs as compared to conventional photosensitizer protoporphyrin IX (PpIX). In-vitro and In-vivo studies show that these GQDs are highly efficient for PDT [15].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) or Graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) are zero dimensional graphene or graphene oxide nanomaterial with excellent photoluminescence (PL) properties which is predominantly due to surface states [16]. The GQDs/GOQDs show enormous potential towards light-emitting diodes [17], bioimaging [18], biosensing [19], heavy metal ion sensing [18], multi-color fluorescence [20], photocatalysis [21], electrochemical biosensing [22], photodynamic therapy [15], etc. In this work, GOQDs are prepared from graphite rod by electrochemical exfoliation method. The electrolyte used is combination of citric acid, sodium hydroxide and hydrogen peroxide. The details of synthesis procedure is discussed in our previous communication [23]. The present methodology provides an easy way towards the synthesis of GOQDs. Here, due to the presence of H2O2, the OH● radicals along with OH− ions cut, oxidize and exfoliate the graphite rod into GOQDs. The produced GOQDs are in the size range of ca. 1.5–5.5 ​nm, and show excitation dependent PL behavior. The prepared GQDs and GOQDs show high biocompatibility and easy cellular uptake which shows their potential towards bioimaging. In this study, the GQDs and GOQDs are used as photosensitizer in photodynamic therapy. The novelty of photodynamic therapy study in this work is the use of micro-watt power UV tubelight as a radiation source instead of using milli-watt power laser and observed high cell killing efficiency. More than 90% of the cells are killed in 5 ​min of UV light exposure.

The preparation and characterization of GQDs/GOQDs have been reported by us recently [23]. For PDT studies we used similar GQDs and GOQDs with minor changes in protocol. For the readers convenience, we assign, the prepared GOQDs in the current study as GQD1, the previously reported GQDs as GQD2 (electrolyte used was 0.1 ​M citric acid and 0.3 ​M NaOH) and previously reported GOQDs as GQD3 (electrolyte used was 0.1 ​M citric acid and 0.15 ​M NaOH). The preparation and characterization of GQD2 and GQD3 is described in our previous communication [23]. Here, we used GQD1, GQD2 and GQD3 for PDT studies.

The chemical reaction of citric acid with NaOH and H2O2 results in formation of sodium citrate salt, H+, OH− ions and OH● radicals.
Image 1

The OH− ions and OH● radicals cut, oxidize and exfoliate the graphite rod into small size GOQDs. The mechanism of preparation of GQDs/GOQDs is discussed in detail in our previous communication [23]. We refer the prepared GOQDs as GQD1 in the text.

Fig. 1 (a, b) illustrate the XPS survey and magnified C1s spectra of GQD1. Fig. 1 (a) shows peaks for C1s at 284 ​eV and O1s at 531.2 ​eV respectively. The atomic percent of carbon and oxygen observed is 45.9% and 54.1% respectively. The magnified C1s spectra of GQD1 is fitted by four deconvoluted peaks. The obtained four peaks correspond to binding energy of sp3 C–C at 284.2 ​eV, sp2 CC at 284.6 ​eV, C–O at 286.0 ​eV and CO at 287.9 ​eV [24]. Broad and intense peaks of C–O and CO indicates that the prepared GQD1 have high amount of oxygen rich functional group. Fig. 1 (c) shows the XRD pattern of GQD1 in which a broad peak at 24⁰ correspond to (002) plane and a peak at ca. 9⁰ correspond to oxygen rich functional group [25,26]. Fig. 1 (d) shows the Raman spectrum of GQD1 in which the D band is observed at 1351 ​cm−1, while the G band at 1585 ​cm−1 and the intensity ratio i.e. ID/IG observed is 1.02. D band arises due to disorder present on the surface of GQD1. The edge or discontinuity i.e. bonding and antibonding orbitals result in intervalley backward scattering of A1g phonon at K-point. The surface states i.e. sp3 C–C, sp3 C–O and sp2 CO results in broadening of D band. G band is a characteristic of carbon materials with sp2 C=C bonds. It is attributed to stretching of sp2 C=C bond and arises due to vibration of E2g phonon at Γ-point. The measurement of defects is calculated by intensity ratio of D and G band. The ID/IG obtained is 1.02 [27,28]. It shows that there are various surface states present on GQD1 surface.

Fig. 2 (a) shows TEM image in which size distribution of GQD1 is shown at 10 ​nm scale. Inset show size distribution curve and high resolution image of a GQD1. Size distribution curve shows that the GQD1 in the range of 1.5–5.5 ​nm are formed. The inset image of a GQD1 at 2 ​nm scale shows the interplanar spacing of 0.22 ​nm. This corresponds to spacing between (100) planes in [001] direction in hexagonal lattice of graphene [29]. Fig. 2 (b) shows UV-VIS and PL excitation spectra of GQD1. Inset images show daylight and 365 ​nm UV lamp images of GQD1. An intense absorption peak at 253 ​nm arises due to π → π* transition (i.e. radiative recombination of electron hole pair) of sp2 C=C bonds. A shoulder peak at 365 ​nm arises due to n → π* transition (i.e. localized levels in band gap) of surface functional groups [30,31]. The two peaks in PL excitation spectra at 358 and 374 ​nm are attributed to armchair edges and surface functional groups. The surface states results in blue luminescence when irradiated with 365 ​nm UV lamp [16,32]. Fig. 2 (c) shows excitation dependent PL of GQD1. PL excitation spectra in Fig. 2 (b) shows that the excitation wavelength is observed at 358 and 374 ​nm. In order to collect the PL spectra, the excitation wavelength is varied from 350 ​nm to 510 ​nm with consecutive 20 ​nm gap. Variation in excitation wavelength results a shift in emission wavelength. PL in GQD1 arises due to the surface functional groups [16,32]. Magnified XPS spectra of C1s shows the functional groups. Synthesis procedure plays a key role in obtaining the surface functional groups. In the present work, H2O2 (a strong oxidizer) is used, which results in the formation of C–O and CO functional group on GQD1 surface. Fig. 2 (d) shows fluorescence decay profile (red) and fitting curve (blue) of GQD1 acquired by TCSPC characterization. The GQD1 are excited with 375 ​nm diode laser and fluorescence decay spectra was recorded at 450 ​nm. The fitting of the obtained fluorescence decay curve is done by tri-exponential function (Fit ​= ​A ​+ ​B1 exp (-t/τ1) ​+ ​B2 exp (-t/τ2) ​+ ​B3 exp (-t/τ3)). Here, τ is fluorescence decay time and B is amplitude of respective lifetime. The value of χ2 observed is 1.13 (range of χ2 between 1 and 1.2 provides a good fit). Fitting with tri-exponential function shows that the fluorescence decay has three relaxation times corresponding to three excitons. Three exciton lifetimes (and their corresponding amplitudes) observed are 2.1 ns (44%), 6.4 ns (7%) and 0.54 ns (44%). Two higher lifetimes, i.e. 2.1 and 6.4 ns correspond to functional groups and the lower lifetime, 0.54 ns correspond to the intrinsic state of GQD1. Two excitation peaks in PL excitation spectra in Fig. 2 (b) and two higher lifetimes in fluorescence decay curve indicates the surface functional groups [15,33]. It is observed that the fluorescence is dominated by surface functional groups in comparison to intrinsic state. For the PDT studies, the prepared GOQDs are defined as GQD1.
Singlet oxygen generation by GQD1, GQD2 and GQD3 is measured by ESR spectroscopy. Generation of other reactive oxygen species by GQD1, GQD2 and GQD3 is measured by DCFDA dye assay.

Fig. 3 illustrates ESR spectra of (a) GQD1, (b) GQD2 and (c) GQD3. ESR spectroscopy is used for detecting the generation of singlet oxygen (1O2) by the GQD1–GQD3. When the GQD1–GQD3 absorb energy from the 365 ​nm UV light source, these go to their respective excited energy states. The GQD1–GQD3, then return to their respective ground states by transferring energy to molecular oxygen in triplet (ground) state (3O2). As a result singlet oxygen (1O2) is generated. The generation of singlet oxygen is detected by ESR using a spin trapper 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidone (TEMP). The TEMP reacts with singlet oxygen, and is converted to 2,2,6,6-tetramethyle-4-piperidone-N-oxyl (TEMPO). TEMPO is a stable nitroxyl radical having an unpaired electron which acts as a spin trap in ESR spectroscopy [34]. TEMP solution is added to GQD1, GQD2 and GQD3. Thereafter, the solutions are continuously irradiated with 365 ​nm UV light for 12 ​min. After every 3 ​min, the mixture solution is acquired and ESR signal is recorded at room temperature. The ESR spectra are recorded at the 0th, 3rd, 6th, 9th and 12th min. The ESR response obtained at the different time intervals, within the 12 ​min period of UV irradiation, are plotted together. For GQD1 and GQD2, maximum intensity is observed till 3 ​min of exposure to UV irradiation. Recombination starts taking place once maximum intensity is observed, which is after the 3rd min of exposure to UV irradiation. For GQD3, maximum intensity is observed till 9 ​min of exposure to UV irradiation. Recombination starts taking place once maximum intensity is observed, which is after the 9th min of exposure to UV irradiation. Intense peak is observed for TEMPO with a magnetic field in the range of 332–338 ​mT. After addition of NaN3 (singlet oxygen quenching agent) we found that there is a significant reduction in ESR signal which indicates that ESR spectra is only due to generation of singlet oxygen [35]. Fig. 3(d–f) shows the detection of generation of other reactive oxygen species using DCFDA dye with GQD1, GQD2 and GQD3. Prior and post irradiation, no significant PL peak is observed which confirms that none of the reactive oxygen species are generated. PL excitation wavelength of 495 ​nm was used and emission range was from 500 to 600 ​nm [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-vitro cytotoxicity measurement by SRB assay
ce:section.ce:para: Cytotoxicity of GQD1, GQD2 and GQD3 on B16F10 and MCF-7 ​cell lines is measured by SRB assay. The detailed procedure of cytotoxicity measurement by SRB assay is given in section 4.2 [36].

Fig. 4 shows the cytotoxicity data of GQD1, GQD2 and GQD3 on B16F10 ​cells and MCF-7 ​cells after 24 ​h of incubation. This study shows that all the three GQDs are essentially biocompatible on B16F10 ​cells and MCF-7 ​cells in a fairly wide concentration range.
The cellular uptake of GQD1, GQD2 and GQD3 on B16F10 ​cells and MCF-7 ​cells is measured by confocal microscopy. The detailed procedure of cellular uptake is given in section 4.3 [37].

Fig. 5 shows the cellular uptake of (a) GQD1, (b) GQD2 and (c) GQD3 on B16F10 ​cells. As depicted from Fig. 5, the B16F10 ​cells are incubated with GQD1, GQD2 and GQD3 (see supporting Fig. 1 for MCF-7 ​cells). In-vitro time dependent cellular uptake of the B16F10 ​cells and MCF-7 ​cells are evaluated for 30, 60, 90 and 120 ​min respectively. The fluorescence due to GQD1, GQD2 and GQD3 inside the B16F10 and MCF-7 ​cells is recorded by confocal microscopy. Confocal images clearly indicate that the GQD1, GQD2 and GQD3 are well distributed inside the B16F10 and MCF-7 ​cells [38].

The photodynamic therapy is done on B16F10 and MCF-7 ​cells using GQD1, GQD2 and GQD3. The procedure for PDT is given in section 4.4. The shape and morphology of cells, before and after PDT is shown in supporting Fig. 3. The live dead cell analysis by trypan blue assay is shown in supporting Fig. 4. The quantification of cell death is shown in Fig. 6 .

Supporting Fig. 2 illustrates the in-vitro PDT procedure. The in-vitro PDT procedure is performed on B16F10 and MCF-7 ​cells. Detailed procedure for photodynamic therapy is given in section 4.4 (PDT procedure). We can see that the GQD1–GQD3 are well distributed inside the cytoplasm of the B16F10 ​cells and the MCF-7 ​cells, which is confirmed by confocal microscopy (Fig. 5) [38]. Supporting Fig. 3 (a) and supporting Fig. 3 (b) illustrate morphology of B16F10 ​cells prior to PDT and post PDT respectively; Supporting Fig. 3 (c) and supporting Fig. 3 (d) illustrate morphology of MCF-7 ​cells prior to PDT and post PDT respectively; As depicted in supporting Fig. 3 (a) (supporting Fig. 3 (c)) the B16F10 ​cells (MCF-7 ​cells) have regular shape and morphology prior to PDT. But, the shape and morphology of the two cell lines are significantly changed due to oxidative stress indicating cell death as shown in supporting Fig. 3 (b) (supporting Fig. 3 (d)). Supporting Fig. 4 shows the live dead cell analysis using the trypan blue assay. Trypan blue stains the dead cells, however, the live cells have the intact cell membrane and are not stained by trypan blue [39]. Supporting Fig. 4 (a) shows the control cells, in which none of the cells are stained by trypan blue. Supporting Fig. 4 (b) shows the control cells which were irradiated with 365 ​nm UV light source for 5 ​min. The arrows indicate the live cells and dead cells. It is observed that some of the cells die when exposed to irradiation for 5 ​min. Supporting Fig. 4(c–e) shows the GQD1–GQD3 incubated cells irradiated for 1, 4 and 5 ​min respectively. Cells irradiated for 1 ​min have very few dead cells, however the cells irradiated for 4 and 5 ​min have maximum number of dead cells. The live cells were counted and plotted as a bar graph shown in Fig. 6. Fig. 6(a–c) illustrates PDT for different time intervals on the B16F10 ​cells with the GQD1, GQD2 and GQD3 respectively. Fig. 6(d–f) shows PDT for different time intervals on the MCF-7 ​cells with the GQD1, GQD2 and GQD3 respectively. The control cells in dark were taken as reference (i.e. 100%). The cells treated with GQD1–GQD3, in dark, show very low cytotoxicity. The control cells and the control cells treated with GQD1–GQD3 irradiated for 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 ​min are shown in the bar graph. It is observed that, more than 80% of the control cells were alive when irradiated with UV source for 5 ​min. The cells treated with GQD1–GQD3, less than 10% of the cells are alive when irradiated with UV source for 5 ​min. Fig. 6 and Table 1 depicts the results of PDT on the B16F10 ​cells and MCF-7 ​cells. The B16F10 ​cells treated with GQD1 and GQD3, less than 1% cells are alive with 3 ​min of irradiation. However, the B16F10 ​cells treated with GQD2, 7% cells are alive with 5 ​min of irradiation. This could possibly be due to presence of high amount of functional groups on the surface of GQD1 and GQD3 (GQD1 and GQD3 are GOQDs) which causes the high amount of singlet oxygen generation and resulting in higher efficacy in cell death. The MCF-7 ​cells treated with GQD1–GQD3, ca. 8% of the cells are alive when irradiated with UV source for 5 ​min. The MCF-7 ​cells treated with GQD1 and GQD3, do not show the similar trend as exhibited by B16F10 ​cells. It could possibly be due to the difference in response of different cell types. The cancer cells are killed due to oxidative stress created by singlet oxygen. From supporting Fig. 3, before irradiation the cells have regular shape, however, after irradiation there is shrinkage of cells is observed in both B16F10 ​cells and MCF-7 ​cells. It is observed that the cell death occurred via shrinkage mechanism [14,15]. We have neglected the photothermal effect of GQD1–GQD3, as there is no absorption observed in the NIR region.

In summary, we have developed an easy approach to kill cancer cells by photodynamic therapy using GQDs and GOQDs as photosensitizer and micro-watt power UV tubelight as a radiation source. The GQDs and GOQDs are highly efficient in cell killing with micro-watt power UV irradiation. The GQDs and GOQDs were prepared from graphite rod by electrochemical exfoliation method. The electrolyte was used as combination of citric acid, NaOH and H2O2. The GQDs and GOQDs with the size range of 1.5–5.5 ​nm, show excitation dependent PL behavior. The easy synthesis method and their excellent luminescence properties can be utilized in several biological applications such as bioimaging and photodynamic therapy. The GQDs and GOQDs show very low cytotoxicity towards B16F10 ​cells and MCF-7 ​cells. The GQDs and GOQDs show high cellular uptake and are well distributed inside the cells. PDT was done using GQDs and GOQDs on B16F10 ​cells and MCF-7 ​cells. More than 90% cells were killed in the short time interval of 5 ​min. The GQD/GOQD PDT show several advantages such as high cell killing efficacy, micro-watt power irradiation, less irradiation time and larger exposed area etc. This makes this strategy very easy and effective for cancer treatment. However, the GQD/GOQD PDT is limited to skin related cancers as the depth of penetration of UV irradiation is very less.

The methodology for the preparation of GOQDs is essentially same except the electrolyte [23]. Briefly, graphite rods (dia. ca. 3 ​mm and length ca. 60 ​mm) are kept in a furnace at 1050 ​°C for 5 ​min. Then the graphite rods are taken out and allowed to cool at room temperature and washed with Milli-Q water. These graphite rods are taken as working and counter electrode. Here, the electrolyte used is 0.1 ​M citric acid, 0.3 ​M NaOH and 0.15 ​M ​H2O2 in 50 ​ml Milli-Q water. The electrochemical experiments, i.e. Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) and Chrono-Amperometry are done with CHI660D Electrochemical Workstation. First CV is done from -1V to +1V, to wet the graphite electrodes. Second, the Chrono-Amperometry is done at +10V with the sensitivity (I/V ​= ​0.1) for 0.5 ​h. After completion of the reaction, the electrolyte solution color changes from colorless to bright yellow. 0.15 ​M calcium chloride is added and heated slightly to precipitate calcium citrate. This solution is kept in centrifuge for 15 ​min ​at 10,000 ​rpm. Then the supernatant is filtered for 7 days by membrane filtration (Himedia, Dialysis membrane-150, LA401, pore size ~2.4 ​nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity of GQDs/GOQDs on cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of GQD1, GQD2 and GQD3 on skin cancer cells (B16F10) and breast cancer cells (MCF-7) were assessed by SRB assay [36]. The B16F10 and MCF-7 ​cells were seeded into 96-well plate, with 1 ​× ​104 ​cells per well, for a period of 24 ​h. Different concentrations of GQD1 were prepared by serially dilution using Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM). The different concentrations of GQD1 were added to the B16F10 ​cells and MCF-7 ​cells, and incubated for 24 ​h in 5% CO2 incubator maintained at 37 ​°C. Thereafter, the B16F10 ​cells and MCF-7 ​cells were fixed with 10% Trichloroacetic acid (TCA) solution. Then the B16F10 ​cells and MCF-7 ​cells were stained with 0.057% SRB for 30 ​min and washed with 1% acetic acid several times. The SRB dye bound to the B16F10 ​cells and MCF-7 ​cells was extracted using 10 ​mM Tris-Buffer solution (pH 10.5). Thereafter, the photometric measurement was done in plate reader measuring absorbance at 560 ​nm. The same procedure is followed for GQD2 and GQD3.

The cell viability percentage was calculated by the formula:
(1)
Cell ​viability
( % )
=
Absorbance ​of ​cells ​treated ​with ​GQDs
Absorbance ​of ​control
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake of GQDs/GOQDs on cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: The cellular uptake of GQD1, GQD2 and GQD3 on skin cancer cells (B16F10) and breast cancer cells (MCF-7) was obtained by confocal microscopy. B16F10 ​cells and MCF-7 ​cells were incubated in the humidified environment maintained at 37 ​°C and 5% CO2. The B16F10 ​cells and MCF-7 ​cells were cultured in DMEM, supplemented with 10% Fetal Bovine Serum and 1% Antibiotic. The cellular uptake of GQD1 was evaluated on B16F10 ​cells and MCF-7 ​cells for different time duration and was analyzed by confocal microscopy. 1 ​× ​104 ​cells were seeded on cover slip in each well of 12 well plate. After 24 ​h, the B16F10 ​cells and MCF-7 ​cells were washed with Phosphate Buffer saline (PBS). Thereafter, 200 ​μl DMEM +200 ​μl of 0.46 ​μM GQD1 (effective concentration of GQD1 ​= ​0.23 ​μM) was added to each well (see supporting information Section 1 for concentration measurement of GQD1–GQD3). After every 30 ​min of incubation, the B16F10 ​cells and MCF-7 ​cells were taken out from the incubator and washed with PBS in order to remove unbound GQD1. The B16F10 ​cells and MCF-7 ​cells were fixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde for 10 ​min in the incubator [37]. Thereafter, the B16F10 ​cells and MCF-7 ​cells were fixed on glass slides with 1% glycerol solution. The maximum cellular uptake time used was 2 ​h with an interval of 30 ​min. The B16F10 ​cells and MCF-7 ​cells were then irradiated and imaged using olympus inverted confocal microscope (Model IX-81) with 488 ​nm multi-argon laser (PMT 496, gain 4.4, offset 2). The same procedure was followed for GQD2 and GQD3 on B16F10 ​cells and MCF-7 cancer cells.

Scheme for PDT is given in supporting Fig. 2. B16F10 cancer cells were grown in 12 petri dishes, each having a size of 35 ​mm. When each Petri dish becomes 80–85% confluent, the DMEM was discarded. For the first six petri dishes (set 1), fresh 2 ​ml of DMEM solution (control) was added. For remaining 6 petri dishes (set 2), a combination of 1 ​ml DMEM and 1 ​ml of 0.46 ​μM GQD1 (control ​+ ​GQD1) (effective concentration of GQD1 ​= ​0.23 ​μM) was added. All the petri dishes were placed in a CO2 incubator for 4 ​h. One Petri dish (dark) from both set 1 and set 2 was not exposed to the UV irradiation. Then, a Petri dish from each of the set 1 and the set 2 were removed from the incubator and irradiated with the 365 ​nm UV light for 1 ​min ​at the distance of 4 ​cm. The power consumed by the UV light was 290 ​μW ​at the distance of 4 ​cm (The intensity observed was 1.03 ​mW/cm2). Subsequently, the remaining petri dishes were removed from each set and was irradiated for 2, 3, 4, 5 ​min respectively. Post irradiation, the petri dishes were placed in the incubator for 4 ​h. After 4 ​h, the petri dishes were removed from the incubator, and the solution in the petri dishes of set 1 (2 ​ml of DMEM solution) and set 2 (combination of 1 ​ml DMEM and 1 ​ml of 0.46 ​μM GQD1) (effective concentration of GQD1 ​= ​0.23 ​μM) was discarded. The B16F10 ​cells attached to the petri dishes of set 1 and set 2 were retained. 400 ​μl of trypsin was added to each Petri dish and kept in the incubator for 1.5–2 ​min. Thereafter, 600 ​μl DMEM was added to the Petri dish to neutralize the effect of trypsin. The B16F10 ​cells attached to the petri dishes were removed by thoroughly mixing it. Then the B16F10 ​cells were placed in a 2 ​ml centrifuge tube and 40 ​μl of Trypan Blue is added and mixed thoroughly [39]. 20 ​μl of this solution was placed in a Hemocytometer. Thereafter, the B16F10 ​cells were counted using microscope at 10× magnification. The same procedure was followed using GQD2 and GQD3 on B16F10 and MCF-7 cancer cell lines.

Jasco V-650 spectrophotometer (Jasco Int. Pvt. Ltd.) was utilized to record the UV-VIS absorption spectra. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra was obtained by Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded by Panalytical X’pert pro Model Empyrean X-ray diffraction system (45 ​kV, 40 ​mA, Cu Kα radiation). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using JEOL JEM-2100F operated at 200 ​kV, was utilized to obtain particle size and distribution. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were collected by scanning XPS microscope (PHI 5000 VersaProbe-II, ULVAC-PHI; using monochromatic Al Kα source with radiation energy of 1486.7 ​eV). Raman spectra measurement were obtained using a LabRAM HR 800 micro-Raman microscope (514 ​nm Argon laser, 10 ​mW). The nanosecond lifetime of GOQDs were measured by time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) spectrometer instrument (IBH Horiba Jobin Yvon, FluoroCube). Horiba NanoLED 375 ​nm diode laser was used to excite the GOQDs and fitting of decay curve was done by IBH DAS 6.2 software. Electron spin resonance spectroscopy (ESR) was done using JEOL, JES-FA200 ESR Spectrometer. Sulforhodamine-B (SRB) assay was used to identify cytotoxicity of GQDs/GOQDs. Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) images were obtained by Olympus inverted confocal microscope (Model IX-81). PDT experiments were done at room temperature with 365 ​nm UV tube light (Philips, TUV 25W/G25T8). Trypan-Blue assay was used for counting of live/dead cells by Hemocytometer.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to express acknowledgment for the financial support by the Wadhwani Research Center for Bioengineering (WRCB) IIT Bombay and the Nanomission, Department of Science and Technology (DST), Government of India. We would like to acknowledge Industrial Research and Consultancy Center (IRCC) Central Facility, IIT Bombay; Sophisticated Analytical Instrument Facility (SAIF), IIT Bombay for TEM, Raman, ESR and Confocal spectroscopy facility; Center of Excellence in Nanoelectronics (CEN), IIT Bombay for XPS facility. We thank Prof. A. Datta, Department of Chemistry, IIT Bombay, for TCSPC facility. We acknowledge Fiji software for confocal image analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

For GQD1–GQD3, Concentration measurement of by Beer Lambert law. Figures for, Cellular uptake of GQD1–GQD3 on MCF-7 ​cells; In-vitro PDT procedure.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jssc.2019.121107.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: World Health Organization, G. H. O., Cancer Facts, http://www.who.int/cancer/en/.
